name: Science
description: Explore physics, chemistry, biology, and the wonders of our world.

questions.text: What is the chemical symbol for gold?
questions.explanation: Gold's symbol Au comes from its Latin name 'Aurum'.

questions.text: How many bones are in the adult human body?
questions.explanation: The adult human body has 206 bones, reduced from around 270 at birth as some fuse together.

questions.text: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: Plants absorb carbon dioxide and use sunlight to convert it into glucose and oxygen.

questions.text: What is the speed of light in a vacuum?
questions.explanation: Light travels at approximately 299,792 km/s (roughly 300,000 km/s) in a vacuum.

questions.text: Which planet is known as the Red Planet?
questions.explanation: Mars appears red due to iron oxide (rust) on its surface.

questions.text: What is the most abundant gas in Earth's atmosphere?
questions.explanation: Nitrogen makes up about 78% of Earth's atmosphere.

questions.text: What organelle is known as the powerhouse of the cell?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria produce ATP through cellular respiration, providing energy to the cell.

questions.text: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons in its nucleus, giving it an atomic number of 6.

questions.text: Which force keeps planets in orbit around the Sun?
questions.explanation: Gravity is the attractive force between masses that keeps planets in elliptical orbits around the Sun.

questions.text: What is the chemical formula for water?
questions.explanation: Water is composed of two hydrogen atoms bonded to one oxygen atom, giving it the formula H2O.

questions.text: Which blood type is known as the universal donor?
questions.explanation: O negative blood lacks A, B, and Rh antigens, making it safe to transfuse to any patient.

questions.text: What is the unit of electric current?
questions.explanation: The ampere (A) is the SI unit of electric current, measuring the flow of electric charge.

questions.text: How many chromosomes do humans normally have?
questions.explanation: Humans have 46 chromosomes arranged in 23 pairs in most body cells.

questions.text: What is the hardest natural substance on Earth?
questions.explanation: Diamond scores 10 on the Mohs hardness scale, making it the hardest known natural substance.

questions.text: What type of animal is a dolphin?
questions.explanation: Dolphins are marine mammals that breathe air, are warm-blooded, and nurse their young with milk.

questions.text: Which element has the chemical symbol 'Fe'?
questions.explanation: Iron's symbol Fe derives from its Latin name 'Ferrum'.

questions.text: What is the process by which a liquid turns into a gas?
questions.explanation: Evaporation is the process by which molecules at a liquid's surface gain enough energy to escape as gas.

questions.text: What is Newton's first law of motion also called?
questions.explanation: Newton's first law states that objects remain at rest or in motion unless acted upon by a force, which is the definition of inertia.

questions.text: What is the pH of a neutral solution at 25°C?
questions.explanation: A pH of 7 indicates a neutral solution at 25°C, where hydrogen and hydroxide ion concentrations are equal.

questions.text: Which part of the brain controls balance and coordination?
questions.explanation: The cerebellum coordinates voluntary movements, balance, and fine motor control.

questions.text: What is the half-life of Carbon-14?
questions.explanation: Carbon-14 has a half-life of approximately 5,730 years, making it useful for dating organic materials.

questions.text: What type of bond holds the two strands of DNA together?
questions.explanation: Hydrogen bonds between complementary base pairs hold the two DNA strands together in the double helix.

questions.text: What is the name of the process by which cells divide to produce identical daughter cells?
questions.explanation: Mitosis produces two genetically identical daughter cells from one parent cell and is used for growth and repair.

questions.text: Which layer of Earth's atmosphere contains the ozone layer?
questions.explanation: The ozone layer sits within the stratosphere, roughly 15–35 km above Earth's surface, absorbing UV radiation.

questions.text: What is Avogadro's number?
questions.explanation: Avogadro's number (6.022 × 10²³) is the number of particles in one mole of a substance.

questions.text: What is the powerhouse reaction that fuels the Sun?
questions.explanation: The Sun generates energy through nuclear fusion, primarily fusing hydrogen nuclei into helium.

questions.text: Which type of electromagnetic radiation has the shortest wavelength?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays have the shortest wavelength and highest frequency in the electromagnetic spectrum.

questions.text: What is the name for the tendency of water molecules to stick to each other?
questions.explanation: Cohesion refers to water molecules attracting each other via hydrogen bonds, which gives water high surface tension.

questions.text: In genetics, what does 'dominant' mean when referring to an allele?
options: It is more common in the population
It is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype even when only one copy (heterozygous) is present.

questions.text: What is the SI unit of pressure?
questions.explanation: The Pascal (Pa) is the SI unit of pressure, defined as one newton per square meter.

questions.text: Which scientist proposed the theory of general relativity?
questions.explanation: Albert Einstein published his general theory of relativity in 1915, describing gravity as curvature of spacetime.

questions.text: What type of rock is formed from cooled magma or lava?
questions.explanation: Igneous rocks form when magma (underground) or lava (surface) cools and solidifies.

questions.text: How many chambers does the human heart have?
questions.explanation: The human heart has four chambers: two atria and two ventricles that pump blood through the body.

questions.text: What is the chemical formula for glucose?
questions.explanation: Glucose (C6H12O6) is a simple sugar and the primary source of energy for cellular respiration.

questions.text: What phenomenon explains why the sky appears blue?
questions.explanation: Rayleigh scattering causes shorter (blue) wavelengths of sunlight to scatter more than longer wavelengths, making the sky appear blue.

questions.text: What is the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle?
options: Energy and mass cannot be simultaneously measured
Position and momentum of a particle cannot both be precisely known at the same time
Light behaves as both wave and particle
Entropy always increases in isolated systems
questions.explanation: The Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle states that the more precisely a particle's position is known, the less precisely its momentum can be known, and vice versa.

questions.text: What is the name of the protein that carries oxygen in red blood cells?
questions.explanation: Hemoglobin is an iron-containing protein in red blood cells that binds and transports oxygen throughout the body.

questions.text: Which subatomic particle has no electric charge?
questions.explanation: Neutrons are found in the nucleus and carry no electric charge, unlike protons (positive) and electrons (negative).

questions.text: What is the second law of thermodynamics concerned with?
options: Entropy always increases in isolated systems
questions.explanation: The second law of thermodynamics states that entropy (disorder) in an isolated system always tends to increase over time.

questions.text: Which scientist is credited with discovering penicillin?
questions.explanation: Alexander Fleming discovered penicillin in 1928 when he observed that Penicillium mold inhibited bacterial growth.

questions.text: What is the Krebs cycle also known as?
questions.explanation: The Krebs cycle (citric acid cycle) is a series of chemical reactions used by aerobic organisms to generate energy from acetyl-CoA.

questions.text: What is the main difference between nuclear fission and fusion?
options: Fission splits heavy nuclei; fusion combines light nuclei
Fission combines light nuclei; fusion splits heavy nuclei
Fission uses protons; fusion uses neutrons
Fission releases no energy; fusion does
questions.explanation: Nuclear fission splits heavy atomic nuclei (like uranium), while fusion joins light nuclei (like hydrogen) together—both release enormous energy.

questions.text: What is a quantum of light called?
questions.explanation: A photon is the quantum (particle) of electromagnetic radiation, including visible light.

questions.text: What process do bacteria use to transfer genetic material between cells without reproduction?
questions.explanation: Bacteria transfer genetic material via transduction (phage-mediated), transformation (uptake of free DNA), and conjugation (direct cell contact).

questions.text: What type of chemical bond involves the sharing of electrons between atoms?
questions.explanation: Covalent bonds form when atoms share one or more pairs of electrons to achieve a stable electron configuration.

questions.text: Which theory in geology describes the movement of Earth's lithospheric plates?
questions.explanation: Plate tectonics describes how Earth's lithosphere is divided into plates that move, causing earthquakes, volcanoes, and mountain formation.

questions.text: What is the name of the force carrier particle for the strong nuclear force?
questions.explanation: Gluons are the force carrier particles of the strong nuclear force, holding quarks together inside protons and neutrons.

questions.text: In which phase of mitosis do chromosomes line up along the cell's equator?
questions.explanation: During metaphase, chromosomes align along the metaphase plate (cell's equator) before being pulled apart.

questions.text: What is the Chandrasekhar limit?
options: The maximum brightness of a supernova
The maximum mass of a stable white dwarf star
The minimum mass for a black hole
The distance at which gravity equals zero
questions.explanation: The Chandrasekhar limit (~1.4 solar masses) is the maximum mass a white dwarf star can have without collapsing into a neutron star.

questions.text: What is CRISPR-Cas9 primarily used for in biology?
options: Editing specific genes in a genome
questions.explanation: CRISPR-Cas9 is a gene-editing tool that allows scientists to precisely cut and modify specific sequences in a genome.
